title: The plus sign has two jobs
steps.0.text: Convert the three pieces of text into numbers with `Number` and name them `sold`, `price` and `tips`. Exactly those names — the checks look for them letter by letter.
steps.1.text: Rewrite the three sums using the new names. The console should show 3741, 1250 and 3759.
checkLabels.t3: The cup count is still text
why.empty: script.js is empty. You deleted everything instead of fixing it.
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: Look at the second and third numbers in the console. One is five characters long, the other six.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel on the right.
why.erased-source: You removed the quotes from the data.
hint2.erased-source: The numbers arrive as text and that does not change. The task is to convert them, not to rewrite where they come from.
hint3.erased-source: Every form field returns text. You type 42, you get `"42"`. You cannot fix that at the source — you fix it where you use it.
why.not-converted: There is no name `sold`, `price` or `tips` holding a real number.
hint2.not-converted: `Number` takes text and pulls the number out of it.
hint3.not-converted: `Number("42")` returns 42 — a real number, not text that looks like one.
hint4.not-converted: Make one new name for each of the three texts. Exactly `sold`, `price` and `tips`.
why.still-glued: The sum still joins instead of adding.
hint2.still-glued: 1247 and 3 added together make 1250. If you get 12473, the plus sign has joined two pieces of text.
hint3.still-glued: The plus sign looks at what you give it. Two numbers — it adds. Text on either side — it joins.
why.tips-glued: The sum with tips is not 3759.
hint2.tips-glued: Here the left side is ALREADY the number 3741. And text still comes out.
hint3.tips-glued: One piece of text on either side is enough. It does not matter that the other side is a proper number — the plus joins them both.
hint4.tips-glued: Convert the tips as well, not just the first two.
hint2.no-total: 1247 times 3. The multiplication worked before too — do not break it.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
blocks.1.text: Look at `script.js`. This time the numbers are in quotes — the way they arrive from any form field. Type 42 into a field and you get the text `"42"`.
blocks.3.text: The first number is right. The other two are not. All three lines look written the same way.
blocks.5.text: An operator between two things is called *binary*. The same sign in front of one thing is *unary* — and then it is a different operator that happens to be written the same way.
blocks.7.text: A minus between two numbers subtracts. In front of one number it flips the sign. Nobody gets confused, because the two jobs look different. With the plus they do not.
blocks.9.text: Between two numbers it adds. The moment there is text on one side, it stops adding and starts joining:
blocks.11.text: It does not matter which side the text is on. The other one turns into text and the two get stuck together.
blocks.13.text: The same three things, a different order, a different result. Operations run left to right: the first line adds 4 and 5, then sticks `px` on. The second starts with text, so everything after it is joining. Once it becomes text, it stays text to the end of the line.
blocks.14.text: The other signs have no second job
blocks.15.text: Multiplication, subtraction and division cannot join anything. So they turn the text into a number and calculate:
blocks.17.text: The same text three times, three numbers, and on the fourth — joining. That is why the first sum at the start of the lesson is right and the other two are not. It is not an accident. The plus sign is busy.
blocks.19.text: Do not rely on the language guessing. Say explicitly what you want:
blocks.21.text: `Number` takes text and pulls the number out. The third line proves it — the result is of kind number, not text that looks like a number.
blocks.23.text: Empty text becomes **zero**, not an error. Spaces only — also zero. So an empty form field does not crash: it quietly enters the sum as nothing and the report simply comes out smaller.
blocks.24.text: `"42px"` is different — there are letters in it and `Number` gives up with `NaN`. There is also `parseInt`, which returns 42 in that same case. `NaN` and the difference between the two have a whole lesson in the next section.
blocks.26.text: You will meet this form too. A plus in front of one piece of text does the same as `Number`, only shorter:
blocks.28.text: It works, it is standard, and you will see it in other people's code. Here we write the long form, because `Number(soldText)` reads and `+soldText` looks like a typo. Know it so you recognize it.
blocks.29.text: Why this is the most common mistake
blocks.30.text: Everything that comes from outside is text. Forms, addresses, files, answers from a server. The numbers in them look like numbers and behave like numbers in every operation but one.
blocks.31.text: So your code will work until somebody tries to add. Then the sum becomes a join, and nothing breaks.
blocks.32.quote: Convert at the door, not halfway down. Let text get inside your code and it will surface where you least expect it.
blocks.34.items.0: The binary plus adds numbers and joins text. One piece of text on either side is enough.
blocks.34.items.1: Operations run left to right. Once it becomes text, it stays text.
blocks.34.items.2: Only the plus is ambiguous. `*`, `-` and `/` always convert to numbers.
blocks.34.items.3: `Number` converts explicitly. Empty text gives 0, text with letters gives `NaN`.
blocks.34.items.4: A plus in front of one piece of text does the same, only shorter. We write the long form.
walkthrough.0.text: The first three lines are not touched. The data arrives as text and that does not change — you cannot fix the form it comes from. That is why three of the checks count the quotes: remove them and the lesson is passed by deletion, and `Number` stays unlearned.
walkthrough.1.text: The conversion happens **once, at the door**, into three new names. The other way — `Number(soldText) * Number(priceText)` straight in the sum — gives the same result but repeats the conversion on every line. One source of text, one place to convert it.
walkthrough.2.text: `const total = sold * price;` — this sum was right before too. Multiplication has no second job and simply turns the text into a number. It is here deliberately: it shows that the whole language is not unreliable, just one operator.
walkthrough.3.text: `const together = sold + price;` — this is the line the lesson exists for. It used to give `"12473"`, five characters side by side. Now it gives 1250.
walkthrough.4.text: `const withTips = total + tips;` — here is the second blow. The left side `total` was **already** the number 3741 and the result still came out as `"374118"`. One piece of text on the right is enough. That is why the conversion goes at the door, not where the problem showed up.
walkthrough.5.text: The three `console.log` lines stay three and print the names, not the sums. The sums already have names, and a name says what the number is. `console.log(sold * price)` would give the same number and nothing more.
